informal: Show that the collection $$\mathcal{T}_\infty = \{U | X - U \text{ is infinite or empty or all of X}\}$$ does not need to be a topology on the set $X$.
lean:
theorem Munkres_exercise_13_3b : ¬ ∀ X : Type, ∀s : Set (Set X),
  (∀ t : Set X, t ∈ s → (Set.Infinite tᶜ ∨ t = ∅ ∨ t = ⊤)) →
  (Set.Infinite (⋃₀ s)ᶜ ∨ (⋃₀ s) = ∅ ∨ (⋃₀ s) = ⊤)
reasoning: The informal statement asks to show 𝒯_∞ need not be a topology. The Lean statement asserts that 𝒯_∞ is not always closed under arbitrary unions (negation of a universal closure property). Since 𝒯_∞ always contains ∅ and X by definition, and is always closed under finite intersection (the union of two infinite complements is infinite), the only topology axiom that can fail is closure under arbitrary union. Hence "is not a topology" and "fails closure under arbitrary union" are mathematically equivalent for this specific 𝒯_∞, and the Lean encoding captures exactly the content of the exercise. The types and quantifiers match (universal X, family of sets, conclusion about ⋃₀), and ⊤ for Set X equals univ.
